lines.text: Alright, we're five hours in. {{ELAPSED}} of this thing. The clock reads {{CLOCK}}. Time to get serious. NOFX. 'Linoleum.' Off Punk in Drublic. 1994.
Fat Mike — Michael Burkett — started this band in LA in '83 with Erik Sandin and Eric Melvin. Fairfax High School kids who decided the world needed more rage and less rules. They were right.

Punk in Drublic went gold six years after it dropped. GOLD. Nobody expected that. The label didn't expect it. Fat Mike definitely didn't expect it because he turned down every major label that came knocking. Fat Wreck Chords — his own label. He kept it indie on purpose.

There's this story about the 'Leave It Alone' video from this album. Fat Mike literally said, 'We made this video and decided not to send it to MTV. We just didn't want to be part of that machine.' That's the move. That's the fucking move right there.
Listen to this one and remember: they built an empire without selling out. We're still here. We're still broadcasting. Same principle.
Green Day. 'Basket Case.' Same year. 1994. The album is Dookie. Three weeks. That's all it took.
Billie Joe Armstrong, Mike Dirnt, Tré Cool. They recorded this at Fantasy Studios in Berkeley in September and October of '93. Three weeks and they changed everything.

Billie's father died when he was ten. Mike's mother was a heroin addict — he got adopted by his real parents, a Native American mom and white dad who split when he was seven. These kids wrote about anxiety and boredom and personal wreckage because they *lived* it.

Dookie sold ten million copies in the US. Twenty million worldwide. Most important pop-punk album of all time — that's what they call it now. Back then it was just three kids from the Bay Area who weren't punk enough for the punks and not commercial enough for commercial rock, so they made their own lane.
Armstrong's lyrics — anxiety, mass murder, masturbation, divorce. Real shit. This song is basically a panic attack in four minutes. Play it loud.
The Descendents. 'Suburban Home.' 1982. Off Milo Goes to College. These guys are the reason pop-punk exists. Full stop.

They formed in Manhattan Beach, California in 1977. Started as a power-pop surf-punk thing, then Milo Aukerman joined as a singer and they became something else entirely. Melodic hardcore. Beach Boys vibes wrapped in punk fury.

Milo actually went to college. UC San Diego. Like the album title said he would. That's real. That's not made up. The other guys — one became a fisherman in Oregon, one's still a postal carrier. That's the Descendents. Day jobs. Art. Dignity.

Steven Blush wrote that their cheeky love songs disguised as hardcore blasts became the most aped formula in rock. Every band that came after them copied it. Skate punk, pop-punk, all of it traces back to these guys singing about girls and rejection and their parents.

Also — and I'm not making this up — they recorded their own farts for the title track. Live in the studio. Multiple takes. That's the ethos right there. Completely earnest. Completely ridiculous. No separation.

tracks.title: And We Thought Nation States Were A Bad Idea

lines.text: Propagandhi. 'And We Thought Nation States Were A Bad Idea.' 2005. Off Potemkin City Limits. Canadian punks. Manitoba. This is what happens when you let anarchists near a recording studio.

Chris Hannah and Jord Samolesky started this in Portage la Prairie in 1986. Fat Mike signed them to Fat Wreck Chords after they opened for NOFX in '92. That's the pipeline — punk bands finding punk bands, building the network.

Potemkin City Limits is about global politics, corporate imperialism, environmental collapse. And it rips. The song title — 'And We Thought Nation States Were A Bad Idea' — that's their whole philosophy right there. They don't believe in borders. They don't believe in hierarchy. They just believe in the music as a weapon.

Chris Hannah did this thing where he pretended to be someone named Glen Lambert for the whole album cycle. A prank. Then in 2006 he announced Glen was being dismissed and Chris Hannah was back. That's the kind of weird, anarchist humor they operate with.

Ramsey Kanaan — founder of AK Press, anarchist publishing — he appears on one of their tracks doing the voice of a petroleum company. They donated the proceeds to activist groups. That's integration. That's real.
Dead Milkmen. 'Punk Rock Girl.' 1988. Off Beelzebubba. Philadelphia. This song got MTV rotation. MTV, folks. In 1988.

Rodney Anonymous on vocals, Joe Jack Talcum on guitar, Dave Blood on bass, Dean Clean on drums. And they decided to write a love song about a punk girl. Simple. Genius. Jangly guitar. Thick Philly accents. Sardonic as hell.

The band started in 1979 as a home-recording project. Joe Genaro and his high school friend Garth — they made cassettes, created this fictional band with a backstory, and eventually it became real. The name came from Toni Morrison's Song of Solomon. They took a literary reference and turned it into punk rock.

They wrote about toxic waste, jellyfish afterlifes, UFOs, conspiracy theories. Not politics in the earnest sense — just absurdist weird shit that somehow landed harder because of how seriously they didn't take it. That's a different kind of rebellion.
This song made it to MTV and they played it on Club MTV live and it was chaotic. Improvised dialogue. Stage antics. Complete disregard for what MTV wanted. And it worked.

tracks.title: Blister in the Sun
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Blister in the Sun.' 1995. This is a Violent Femmes cover. San Francisco supergroup. Cover band. They took one of the biggest alternative hits ever and punked it into oblivion.

Fat Mike of NOFX, Joey Cape from Lagwagon, Spike Slawson from Swingin' Utters — these guys were in other bands and decided that off-time meant let's cover songs. Have a Ball. That was the first album. 1997. Pop hits from the '60s and '70s, transformed.

They wear matching costumes at every show. Cowboy outfits for their country album. Drag for their Broadway album. They're not trying to be cool. They're trying to be weird and funny and committed to the bit in a way that makes it genuine.

In 2006 they were booked to play three nights at a baseball stadium in Pittsburgh. Got booed on the first night after a Pirates game. Second and third nights cancelled. And they still kept going. That's punk rock.
A cover band that operates with more integrity than most original acts. That's what the Gimmes are. They take songs that don't belong to them and make them more honest somehow.

tickerItems.text: Smoke visibility: Isthmus-wide haze reported. Multiple structure fires active. Recommend windows closed, air filtration engaged. East Side still hottest zone — kill count tracking 12% up YoY.
Beltline corridor remains contested. Vehicle incidents up since midnight hour. SafeWalk reporting 340+ reroutes due to hot zones. Stay home. Stay barricaded.

PurgeShield Panels: Last year 4,200 units sold in Madison metro. Don't let installation errors happen to you. Verify your contractor. Verify your angle. Verify you're still alive in the morning. Call toll-free.
